text: A thirty-eight years old female presented with symptoms of dysmenorrhea and infertility. On ultrasound examination, bilateral endometriomas were found and thereby the decision for laparoscopy was taken.
The laparoscopic view reveals a frozen pelvis with adhesions involving bowel loops, obscuring the visualization of the uterus, bilateral fallopian tubes, and ovaries.
The surgeon begins adhesiolysis, employing traction and counter-traction techniques to carefully separate adhesions in the pelvic region.
The surgeon begins adhesiolysis by applying traction from a normal area towards an abnormal area, revealing a white line that indicates a clear dissection plane between the two structures.
During the adhesiolysis procedure for endometriosis, the surgeon uses cold scissors to carefully dissect adhesions, aiming to spare the peritoneum and minimize lateral thermal damage.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to dissect in the correct planes, carefully avoiding adhesions and preventing blood loss while ensuring no injury to adjacent structures.

The surgeon visualizes the uterus and prepares to inject vasopressin into the myelodendron, using a dilution of twenty units in one hundred ml or forty units in two hundred ml, to aid in adhesiolysis and reduce blood loss.
